name: Anxiety Disorder - Comprehensive Multi-Query
description: Comprehensive test for the PheKB anxiety phenotype algorithm. A psychiatrist needs to identify their complete anxiety patient population for a treatment outcomes study. This requires searching both Condition resources (formal diagnoses) and MedicationRequest resources (anti-anxiety prescriptions), because some patients may be prescribed medications by primary care without a formal anxiety diagnosis in the psychiatry system. Tests whether LLMs can construct multiple FHIR queries across resource types to capture the full cohort.
prompt: Find all patients with an anxiety disorder. Search both diagnoses and anti-anxiety medication prescriptions to capture the complete population.

prompts.naive: Find all patients with an anxiety disorder. Search both diagnoses and anti-anxiety medication prescriptions to capture the complete population.
prompts.expert: A psychiatrist is conducting a treatment outcomes study and needs to identify their complete anxiety patient population. They explain that while many patients have a formal anxiety diagnosis on record, some patients are prescribed anti-anxiety medications such as sertraline, escitalopram, buspirone, or alprazolam by their primary care provider without a formal anxiety diagnosis appearing in the psychiatry system. The psychiatrist needs to find all patients who either have an anxiety disorder diagnosis or have been prescribed any of these anti-anxiety medications.

metadata.algorithm_path_description: Full PheKB anxiety case identification combining diagnosis codes and medication evidence. Requires multi-query strategy: Condition search for anxiety diagnoses plus MedicationRequest search for anti-anxiety medications to catch patients without formal diagnosis codes.

expected_query.notes: This test case expects MULTIPLE queries (see metadata.expected_queries). The expected_query.url is the minimum viable query. Evaluation unions patient IDs from both queries to get the complete anxiety population including patients with medications but no formal diagnosis.
